name: Fear and Mental Stress
description: Rules for handling fear effects, mental stress, and their prolonged consequences in D&D campaigns
preamble: Due to the nature of their vocation, adventurers tend to be less susceptible to fear and mental stress than common folk. Whereas a farmer might flee in terror from a bear or an apparition, adventurers are made of sterner stuff. That said, certain creatures and game effects can terrify or fray the mind of even the most stalwart adventurer.
important_note: If you plan to use any of these rules, discuss them with your players at the start of the campaign

fear_effects.title: Fear Effects
fear_effects.description: Whenever the characters encounter something that is supernaturally frightful, use the Frightened condition as the baseline effect
fear_effects.baseline: Fear effects typically require a Wisdom saving throw, with a save DC set based on how terrifying the situation is

examples.example: When the characters open a sarcophagus, a harmless yet terrifying apparition appears
A character triggers a magical trap that creates an illusory manifestation of that character's worst fears, visible only to that character
A portal to the Abyss opens, revealing a nightmarish realm of torment and slaughter

typical_mechanics: Typically, a Frightened creature repeats the saving throw at the end of each of its turns, ending the effect on itself on a success

additional_effects.description: At your discretion, a Frightened creature might be subject to other effects as long as the Frightened condition lasts

examples.description: The Frightened creature must take the Dash action on each of its turns and uses its movement to get farther away from the source of its fear
Attack rolls against the Frightened creature have Advantage
The Frightened creature can do only one of the following on each of its turns: move, take an action, or take a Bonus Action

mental_stress_effects.title: Mental Stress Effects
mental_stress_effects.description: When a character is subjected to an effect that causes intense mental stress, Psychic damage is the best way to emulate that effect
mental_stress_effects.mechanics: Mental stress can usually be resisted with a successful Wisdom save, but sometimes an Intelligence or Charisma save is more appropriate
mental_stress_effects.partial_resistance: On a successful save, a character might take half as much damage instead of no damage, at your discretion

examples.example: A character ingests a hallucinogenic substance that distorts the character's perception of reality
A character touches a fiendish idol that tears at the character's mind, threatening to shatter it
A magical trap flings a character into the Far Realm until the end of that character's next turn

prolonged_effects.title: Prolonged Effects
prolonged_effects.description: Exposure to mental stress can cause prolonged effects

short_term_effects.name: Short-Term Effects
short_term_effects.effect: The character has the Frightened, Incapacitated, or Stunned condition
manifestations: Might be accompanied by alarming behavior
removal: These effects can be suppressed with the Calm Emotions spell
These effects can be removed by the Lesser Restoration spell

long_term_effects.name: Long-Term Effects
long_term_effects.effect: The character has Disadvantage on some or all ability checks
long_term_effects.cause: Stemming from an unwillingness or inability to exert a particular set of abilities

examples.example: The character might feel enervated and unable to exert much Strength
The character might become so suspicious of others that Charisma checks are more difficult

removal: These effects can be suppressed with the Calm Emotions spell
These effects can be removed by the Lesser Restoration spell

indefinite_effects.name: Indefinite Effects
indefinite_effects.description: An indefinite effect is a long-term effect that lasts until removed by a Greater Restoration spell
indefinite_effects.suppression: It can be suppressed by a Calm Emotions spell

contains: Mental stress effects and Psychic damage
Spell interactions with fear and stress
